name: Google Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Protocol
pricing_notes: Open protocol specification; SDKs free; underlying compute costs vary by implementation

auth.notes: Protocol supports multiple auth schemes; agent card declares supported auth methods

performance.notes: Protocol overhead minimal; actual latency dominated by agent task execution

agent_readiness.notes: Google's open standard for agent-to-agent communication. Agent Cards (JSON metadata about agent capabilities). Task lifecycle: submitted → working → completed/failed. SSE streaming for live updates. Complements MCP (MCP handles tool access; A2A handles agent-to-agent delegation).

gotchas: Very early protocol (0.x) — spec and SDK evolving rapidly
Small ecosystem — few agents implement A2A yet
Python and JS SDKs available; other languages need custom implementation
Complements rather than competes with MCP — teams need both
Trust and authentication between agents is complex to implement correctly
best_when: Building multi-agent systems where agents need to delegate tasks to specialized agents
Enterprise scenarios requiring standardized agent-to-agent communication
Horizontal agent scaling (agent orchestrators calling worker agents)
Interoperability between agents from different vendors/teams
avoid_when: Tool calling / data access (use MCP instead)
summary: Google A2A (Agent-to-Agent) is an open protocol standard for agent interoperability, enabling agents from different frameworks to communicate via standardized task delegation. Agent Cards declare capabilities; tasks flow through a lifecycle with SSE streaming. Designed to complement MCP (MCP = tools/data, A2A = agent delegation). Very early-stage but backed by Google and growing vendor adoption.
